1:
"Brethren, my heart's desire and prayer to God for Israel is, that they might be saved." The witnesses note Paul's heartfelt desire and prayer for Israel's salvation. Chrysostom notes Paul, about to rebuke Israel more vehemently, first removes every suspicion of hatred — for it is not likely that the same person should desire their salvation, pray for it, and yet hate them; he calls his exceeding desire and prayer his "heart's desire." The witnesses note Paul declaring his heart's desire and prayer to God for Israel — that they might be saved; the apostle's deep love for his own people, even amid their unbelief and his coming rebuke; the longing of the gospel heart for the salvation of the lost, the rebuke flowing from love. The witnesses note the heart's desire. The witnesses note prayer for Israel's salvation.

The witnesses note Paul's heart's desire and prayer for Israel's salvation — the apostle's deep love for his people, the rebuke flowing from love.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's desire and prayer for Israel. The lesson the expositors draw is the apostle's deep love for his own people, the rebuke flowing from love and longing for their salvation. The witnesses note the desire: "my heart's desire... for Israel" — Chrysostom notes Paul calls his exceeding desire his "heart's desire"; the deep longing; the heartfelt desire; the love for Israel; the yearning of the apostle. The expositors observe the prayer: "and prayer to God for Israel is, that they might be saved" — Paul prays to God for Israel's salvation; the prayer; the intercession; the desire issuing in prayer; the longing brought to God. The witnesses press Chrysostom's note on the removal of hatred: Paul, about to rebuke Israel vehemently, first removes the suspicion of hatred — for one who desires, prays for, and longs for their salvation cannot hate them; the love behind the rebuke; the rebuke not from hatred but love; the desire disproving the enmity. The expositors draw the lesson on the love behind the rebuke: Paul's coming rebuke of Israel's unbelief flows from love — the apostle longs for their salvation; the rebuke of love; the severity from tenderness; the love that rebukes for good; "faithful are the wounds of a friend" (Proverbs 27:6). The witnesses observe the apostle's love for his people: Paul, the apostle of the Gentiles, deeply loved his own people, the Jews — desiring and praying for their salvation; the love for his kinsmen (Romans 9:1-3); the heart for Israel; the apostle's burden. The expositors note the example for the believer: Paul's desire and prayer for the lost is the example — the gospel heart longing and praying for the salvation of the lost; the example of love; the prayer for the unsaved; the burden for souls; the heart of the evangelist. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the heart's desire, the prayer for Israel, and the love behind the rebuke. The verse thus records Paul's heart's desire and prayer for Israel's salvation — teaching the apostle's deep love for his people, the rebuke flowing from love and longing for their salvation.
2:
"For I bear them record that they have a zeal of God, but not according to knowledge." The witnesses note Israel's zeal without knowledge. Gill notes they had a zeal for God — for his being and unity (against the idolatry of the Gentiles), for the word of God, the law (moral and ceremonial), and the worship of God, sparing no pains to make proselytes; the apostle bears them this record. The witnesses note Paul testifying that Israel had a zeal of God, but not according to knowledge; the sincere but misdirected zeal of the Jews, earnest for God yet ignorant of the true way of righteousness; the zeal that, lacking knowledge, opposed the very gospel it should have embraced; the peril of zeal without knowledge. The witnesses note a zeal of God. The witnesses note not according to knowledge.

The witnesses note Israel's zeal of God without knowledge — sincere but misdirected, earnest for God yet ignorant of the true righteousness.

So the witnesses make the verse Israel's zeal without knowledge. The lesson the expositors draw is the peril of sincere but misdirected zeal, earnest for God yet ignorant of the true way of righteousness. The witnesses note the zeal: "they have a zeal of God" — Gill notes Israel had a zeal for God — for his being and unity, his word, his law, his worship, sparing no pains; the zeal; the earnestness; the religious fervour; the zeal for God. The expositors observe the defect: "but not according to knowledge" — the zeal was not according to knowledge — sincere but ignorant, earnest but misdirected; the zeal without knowledge; the defect; the ignorance; the misdirected fervour. The witnesses press the peril of zeal without knowledge: Israel's zeal, lacking knowledge of the true righteousness (v.3), opposed the gospel it should have embraced — persecuting Christ and his church in the name of God; the peril of zeal without knowledge; the sincere error; the earnestness misdirected; "I verily thought... I ought to do many things contrary" (Acts 26:9). The expositors draw the lesson on the insufficiency of zeal: zeal alone is not enough — it must be according to knowledge; the sincere may be sincerely wrong; the earnestness no proof of truth; the zeal that errs; the need of knowledge with zeal. The witnesses observe Paul's fairness: "I bear them record" — Paul fairly acknowledges Israel's zeal, gives them their due; the candour; the fair acknowledgment; the apostle's justice even in rebuke; the record borne honestly. The expositors note the warning to the religious: Israel's zeal without knowledge warns the religious — that one may be zealous, sincere, devout, and yet wrong; the warning; the peril of misdirected devotion; the zeal that opposes the truth; the need of true knowledge. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the zeal of God, the not according to knowledge, and the peril of misdirected zeal. The verse thus records Israel's zeal without knowledge — teaching the peril of sincere but misdirected zeal, earnest for God yet ignorant of the true righteousness.
3:
"For they being ignorant of God's righteousness, and going about to establish their own righteousness, have not submitted themselves unto the righteousness of God." The witnesses note Israel's ignorance and self-righteousness. Gill notes they were ignorant of the righteousness of God revealed in the gospel — the righteousness of Christ — and of the spirituality of the law (which requires conformity of heart, not merely outward obedience). The witnesses note Paul exposing the root of Israel's error — being ignorant of God's righteousness (the righteousness of Christ provided in the gospel), and going about to establish their own (by the works of the law), they did not submit to the righteousness of God; the threefold fault — ignorance, self-righteous effort, and refusal to submit — the very anatomy of legalism opposing grace. The witnesses note ignorant of God's righteousness. The witnesses note establishing their own.

The witnesses note Israel ignorant of God's righteousness, establishing their own, refusing to submit — the anatomy of legalism opposing grace.

So the witnesses make the verse Israel's ignorance and self-righteousness. The lesson the expositors draw is the threefold root of Israel's error — ignorance of God's righteousness, the effort to establish their own, and the refusal to submit. The witnesses note the ignorance: "being ignorant of God's righteousness" — Gill notes they were ignorant of the righteousness of God revealed in the gospel (the righteousness of Christ) and of the spirituality of the law; the ignorance; the not knowing the true righteousness; the blindness to God's provision; the root of the error. The expositors observe the self-righteous effort: "going about to establish their own righteousness" — they went about (laboured, strove) to establish their own righteousness by the works of the law; the self-righteousness; the effort to be justified by their own doing; the establishing of their own; the legal striving. The witnesses press the refusal to submit: "have not submitted themselves unto the righteousness of God" — they did not submit to the righteousness of God (the righteousness of Christ received by faith); the non-submission; the refusal; the pride that would not receive the free righteousness; the heart that would not stoop to grace. The expositors draw the lesson on the anatomy of legalism: the threefold fault — ignorance of God's righteousness, the effort to establish their own, the refusal to submit — is the very anatomy of legalism opposing grace; the legalism; the self-righteousness; the pride against grace; the works against faith; the legal heart. The witnesses observe the pride in the non-submission: to submit to God's righteousness requires humility — the confession that one has no righteousness of one's own, the receiving of Christ's; the pride that would not submit; the self-righteousness that would not stoop; the heart that would not own its poverty. The expositors note the contrast of the two righteousnesses: God's righteousness (the gift of Christ, received by faith) and their own (the works of the law) — the two ways; the grace and the works; the gift and the achievement; the righteousness of God and of man. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the ignorance of God's righteousness, the establishing of their own, and the refusal to submit. The verse thus records Israel's ignorance and self-righteousness — teaching the threefold root of legalism: ignorance of God's righteousness, the effort to establish their own, and the refusal to submit to grace.
4:
"For Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to every one that believeth." The witnesses note Christ the end of the law for righteousness. Gill notes "the law" here is the moral law, of which Christ is the end — the end in respect of its perfect fulfilment (he fulfilled it for righteousness), and the end of it as a way of justification, terminating its use for that purpose. The witnesses note Paul declaring that Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to every believer; the rich phrase weighed — Christ the goal to which the law pointed, and the termination of the law as a way of righteousness, having perfectly fulfilled it; in him the believer has the righteousness the law demanded but could not give. The witnesses note Christ the end of the law. The witnesses note righteousness to every believer.

The witnesses note Christ the end of the law for righteousness — the goal the law pointed to and the termination of the law as a way of justification.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ the end of the law for righteousness. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ is the goal to which the law pointed and the termination of the law as a way of righteousness, in whom the believer has the righteousness the law demanded. The witnesses note the declaration: "Christ is the end of the law for righteousness" — Christ is the end (telos) of the law for righteousness; the end; the goal and termination; the law's relation to Christ; the end for righteousness. The expositors weigh the sense of "end" (telos): the witnesses note the senses — Christ the goal/aim to which the law pointed (its types, its demands, all leading to him); Christ the termination of the law as a way of justification (the law no longer a way of righteousness, ended for that purpose); Christ the fulfilment of the law (perfectly keeping it for righteousness); the rich phrase embracing the goal, the termination, and the fulfilment. The witnesses press the chief sense for righteousness: "for righteousness" — Christ is the end of the law specifically for righteousness — the law as a way of justification is ended in Christ, who is himself the righteousness the believer needs; the law's role in justification terminated; Christ the righteousness; the end for justification. The expositors draw the lesson on the law fulfilled and ended in Christ: Christ fulfilled the law (kept it perfectly for righteousness) and so ended it as a way of justification — the believer now justified by his righteousness, not by the law; the law fulfilled and superseded as a way of righteousness; Christ the end; the law's purpose accomplished. The witnesses observe "to every one that believeth": "to every one that believeth" — Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to every believer — the righteousness received by faith, open to all who believe (Jew and Gentile, v.12); the faith the means; the every believer; the universal offer. The expositors note the contrast with v.3: against Israel's effort to establish their own righteousness by the law (v.3), Christ is the end of the law for righteousness — the law cannot justify, but Christ, its end, is the believer's righteousness; the contrast; the law ended, Christ the righteousness; the grace against the works. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Christ the end of the law, the righteousness to every believer, and the law fulfilled and ended in Christ. The verse thus records Christ the end of the law for righteousness — teaching that Christ is the goal the law pointed to and the termination of the law as a way of righteousness, the believer's righteousness by faith.
5:
"For Moses describeth the righteousness which is of the law, That the man which doeth those things shall live by them." The witnesses note the righteousness of the law — do and live. Gill notes the apostle wisely gives the description in the words of Moses, who was held in great esteem among the Jews; the description of the righteousness the law requires is, that the man who does those things shall live by them (Leviticus 18:5). The witnesses note Paul citing Moses on the righteousness of the law — the man who does these things shall live by them; the principle of legal righteousness, that it is earned by doing, the law promising life only to perfect obedience; the standard that, since none perfectly does, leaves all under condemnation and drives to the righteousness of faith. The witnesses note doeth those things. The witnesses note shall live by them.

The witnesses note the righteousness of the law — the doer shall live by them; legal righteousness earned by perfect doing, which none attains.

So the witnesses make the verse the righteousness of the law. The lesson the expositors draw is the principle of legal righteousness — earned by doing, the law promising life only to perfect obedience, which none attains. The witnesses note the citation of Moses: "Moses describeth the righteousness which is of the law" — Gill notes the apostle wisely uses the words of Moses, esteemed among the Jews; the citation; the description from Moses; the authority of Moses; the law's own witness. The expositors observe the principle: "the man which doeth those things shall live by them" — the man who does the things of the law shall live by them (Leviticus 18:5); the principle of legal righteousness — do and live; the doing; the life by the doing; the law's terms. The witnesses press the demand of perfect doing: the law promises life to the one who "doeth those things" — perfectly, continually, all of them; the perfect obedience required; the doing of all; "cursed is every one that continueth not in all things" (Galatians 3:10); the exacting standard. The expositors draw the lesson on the impossibility for sinners: since the law promises life only to perfect doing, and no sinner perfectly does, the law leaves all under condemnation; the impossibility; the standard none meets; the law condemning; the way of life closed by works; the legal righteousness unattainable. The witnesses observe the contrast with faith: the righteousness of the law (do and live) is set against the righteousness of faith (believe and live, v.6-9); the two ways; the doing and the believing; the works and the faith; the law and the gospel; the contrast drawn. The expositors note the driving to faith: the law's demand (do and live), being unattainable, drives the sinner to the righteousness of faith — to Christ, who did and lived, and whose righteousness is received by faith; the law driving to grace; the impossibility leading to Christ; the schoolmaster (Galatians 3:24); the law's demand pointing to faith. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the doeth those things, the shall live by them, and the legal righteousness none attains. The verse thus records the righteousness of the law — do and live — teaching the principle of legal righteousness earned by perfect doing, which none attains, driving to the righteousness of faith.
6:
"But the righteousness which is of faith speaketh on this wise, Say not in thine heart, Who shall ascend into heaven? (that is, to bring Christ down from above:)" The witnesses note the righteousness of faith and the accessible word. Barnes notes Paul does not claim Moses formally described justification by faith, but expresses the language of faith mainly in words Moses used (Deuteronomy 30:12-14), which aptly express its meaning — the thing not remote and difficult, but near. The witnesses note Paul, in the words of Moses, describing the righteousness of faith — it does not say "Who shall ascend into heaven?" (to bring Christ down, as if he had not come), for the work is already done; the contrast with the law's "do and live," the righteousness of faith resting on the finished work of Christ, not on impossible human achievement. The witnesses note the righteousness of faith speaks. The witnesses note not ascend into heaven.

The witnesses note the righteousness of faith — not "Who shall ascend into heaven?" — for Christ has already come; faith rests on the finished work.

So the witnesses make the verse the righteousness of faith and the accessible word. The lesson the expositors draw is that the righteousness of faith rests on the finished work of Christ, not on impossible human achievement. The witnesses note the speaking of faith: "the righteousness which is of faith speaketh on this wise" — the righteousness of faith speaks differently from the law; the language of faith; the way of faith described; the speaking; the contrast with v.5. The expositors observe Barnes's note on the use of Moses: Paul does not claim Moses formally described justification by faith, but expresses the language of faith mainly in words Moses used (Deuteronomy 30:12-14), which aptly express its meaning — the thing near, not remote; the borrowed words; the apt expression; the language of accessibility. The witnesses press the "who shall ascend into heaven?": "Say not in thine heart, Who shall ascend into heaven? (that is, to bring Christ down from above:)" — faith does not say "who shall ascend into heaven to bring Christ down?" — as if Christ had not come; the work already done; Christ has come; no need to bring him down; the incarnation accomplished. The expositors draw the lesson on the finished work: the righteousness of faith rests on the finished work of Christ — he has already come (no need to ascend to bring him down); the work done; faith resting, not achieving; the accomplished redemption; the contrast with the law's impossible doing. The witnesses observe the contrast with the law: the law says "do and live" (an impossible achievement, v.5); faith says the work is done, Christ has come; the contrast; the doing and the done; the achievement and the rest; the law and the gospel. The expositors note the ease of faith: faith does not require the impossible (ascending to heaven, descending to the deep); the word is near (v.8); the way of faith accessible, not remote; the ease; the nearness; the gospel within reach. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the righteousness of faith speaking, the not ascend into heaven, and the finished work of Christ. The verse thus records the righteousness of faith — not "who shall ascend into heaven?" — teaching that faith rests on the finished work of Christ, who has already come, not on impossible human achievement.
7:
"Or, Who shall descend into the deep? (that is, to bring up Christ again from the dead.)" The witnesses note the second impossible question answered by the finished work. Barnes notes these words are also part of the address of Moses (Deuteronomy 30:13), not literally quoted (the Hebrew speaks of "beyond the sea"), but the sense expressed in Moses's words — Paul expressing the language of faith; the words refer to that which is remote and difficult. The witnesses note Paul continuing — faith does not say "Who shall descend into the deep?" (to bring Christ up from the dead), for Christ is already risen; the second impossible achievement set aside, the resurrection accomplished; faith resting on the completed work of incarnation (v.6) and resurrection (v.7), needing to do nothing but believe. The witnesses note who shall descend into the deep. The witnesses note Christ already risen.

The witnesses note faith not saying "Who shall descend into the deep?" — for Christ is already risen; faith rests on the completed work.

So the witnesses make the verse the second impossible question answered by the finished work. The lesson the expositors draw is that faith rests on the completed work — the incarnation and the resurrection accomplished — needing only to believe. The witnesses note the second question: "Who shall descend into the deep? (that is, to bring up Christ again from the dead.)" — faith does not say "who shall descend into the deep to bring Christ up?" — as if Christ were not risen; the second impossible achievement; the descending to the deep; the bringing up of Christ. The expositors observe Barnes's note on the use of Moses: these words are part of Moses's address (Deuteronomy 30:13), not literally quoted (the Hebrew speaks of "beyond the sea"), but the sense expressed in Moses's words; Paul expressing the language of faith; the borrowed words; the apt sense; the remote made near. The witnesses press the resurrection accomplished: faith does not ask who shall bring Christ up from the dead — for Christ is already risen; the resurrection done; no need to descend; Christ raised; the completed resurrection. The expositors draw the lesson on the completed work: faith rests on the completed work — the incarnation (Christ come down, v.6) and the resurrection (Christ raised, v.7) accomplished; the work done; faith resting, not achieving; the redemption complete; the two great acts done. The witnesses observe the needing only to believe: since the work is done (incarnation and resurrection), faith needs to do nothing but believe — not to ascend, not to descend, not to achieve, but to receive; the ease of faith; the believing; the resting on the done work; the nothing-to-do-but-believe. The expositors note the contrast with the law's impossible demand: the law demands impossible doing (do and live, v.5); faith finds the work done (Christ come and risen); the contrast; the impossible achievement and the accomplished work; the doing and the believing; the law and the gospel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the not descend into the deep, the Christ already risen, and the completed work. The verse thus records faith not saying "who shall descend into the deep?" — teaching that faith rests on the completed work, the incarnation and resurrection accomplished, needing only to believe.
8:
"But what saith it? The word is nigh thee, even in thy mouth, and in thy heart: that is, the word of faith, which we preach;" The witnesses note the nearness of the word of faith. Barnes notes "the word is nigh thee" is still the language of Moses (Deuteronomy 30:14); the meaning is that the doctrine is not difficult to be understood and embraced — what is nigh us may be easily obtained. The witnesses note Paul declaring, in Moses's words, that the word is nigh — in the mouth and in the heart — that is, the word of faith which the apostles preach; the gospel near and accessible, not remote like the law's impossible demand, the word of faith brought close, ready to be received by the believing heart and confessed by the mouth; the accessibility of the gospel. The witnesses note the word is nigh thee. The witnesses note the word of faith.

The witnesses note the word of faith near — in the mouth and heart; the gospel accessible, not remote like the law's impossible demand.

So the witnesses make the verse the nearness of the word of faith. The lesson the expositors draw is the accessibility of the gospel — the word of faith brought near, ready to be received by the heart and confessed by the mouth. The witnesses note the nearness: "the word is nigh thee" — Barnes notes this is Moses's language (Deuteronomy 30:14); the word near; the gospel close; the accessibility; what is nigh easily obtained; the word at hand. The expositors observe the location: "even in thy mouth, and in thy heart" — the word is in the mouth (to be confessed) and in the heart (to be believed); the mouth and the heart; the near word; the word within reach; the place of the word. The witnesses press the identification: "that is, the word of faith, which we preach" — the near word is the word of faith which the apostles preach — the gospel; the word of faith; the preached gospel; the doctrine of justification by faith; the message proclaimed. The expositors draw the lesson on the accessibility of the gospel: the gospel is near, accessible — not remote like the law's impossible demand (ascending, descending, doing all); the word of faith brought close, ready to be received; the easy access; the gospel within reach; "the word is nigh thee." The witnesses observe the contrast with the law: the law's righteousness is remote and impossible (do all and live); the word of faith is near (believe and confess); the contrast; the remote and the near; the impossible and the accessible; the law and the gospel. The expositors note the word of faith preached: the word of faith is "which we preach" — the gospel proclaimed by the apostles; the preached word; the means of faith (v.17); the gospel that brings the righteousness near; the word ministered. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the word is nigh thee, the word of faith, and the accessibility of the gospel. The verse thus records the nearness of the word of faith — teaching the accessibility of the gospel, the word brought near, ready to be received by the heart and confessed by the mouth.
9:
"That if thou shalt confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus, and shalt believe in thine heart that God hath raised him from the dead, thou shalt be saved." The witnesses note the confession and faith that save. Gill notes the confession is a good, sincere, hearty confession before the church and the world that Christ is one's Lord and Saviour, arising from a true faith in the heart. The witnesses note Paul declaring that if one confesses with the mouth the Lord Jesus and believes in the heart that God raised him from the dead, he shall be saved; the two joined — the inward faith and the outward confession; the confession of Jesus as Lord and the belief in his resurrection (the proof of his deity and the acceptance of his work) the marks of the saved; the way of salvation stated plainly. The witnesses note confess the Lord Jesus. The witnesses note believe the resurrection.

The witnesses note confession of Jesus as Lord and belief in his resurrection — the outward confession and inward faith that save.

So the witnesses make the verse the confession and faith that save. The lesson the expositors draw is the joining of the inward faith and the outward confession — the belief in the heart and the confession of the mouth that mark the saved. The witnesses note the confession: "if thou shalt confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus" — Gill notes a sincere, hearty confession before the church and the world that Christ is one's Lord and Saviour; the confession; the owning of Jesus as Lord; the outward profession; the mouth's confession. The expositors observe the faith: "and shalt believe in thine heart that God hath raised him from the dead" — the belief in the heart that God raised Jesus from the dead; the inward faith; the belief in the resurrection; the heart's faith; the believing. The witnesses press the joining of the two: "thou shalt be saved" — the confession and the faith joined; the inward faith and the outward confession; the heart believing and the mouth confessing; the two marks of the saved; the way of salvation. The expositors draw the lesson on the confession of Jesus as Lord: to confess "the Lord Jesus" is to own Jesus as Lord — his deity, his authority, his sovereignty; the confession of his Lordship; the owning of him as Master and God; the confession that submits; the Lord Jesus. The witnesses observe the belief in the resurrection: the faith is "that God hath raised him from the dead" — the belief in the resurrection, the central fact; the resurrection the proof of Christ's deity, the acceptance of his sacrifice, the ground of justification (Romans 4:25); the resurrection faith; the central belief. The expositors note the order — faith then confession: the faith is in the heart (the root), the confession with the mouth (the fruit); the inward and the outward; the faith the source, the confession the expression; the heart and the mouth; the believing and the owning. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the confess the Lord Jesus, the believe the resurrection, and the way of salvation. The verse thus records the confession and faith that save — teaching the joining of the inward faith (believing the resurrection) and the outward confession (owning Jesus as Lord) that mark the saved.
10:
"For with the heart man believeth unto righteousness; and with the mouth confession is made unto salvation." The witnesses note the heart's faith unto righteousness and the mouth's confession unto salvation. Gill notes true faith does not lie in the bare assent of the mind, nor in the brain, nor in the tongue, but in the heart — a believing with all the heart, in which all the powers of the soul are concerned. The witnesses note Paul explaining the two — with the heart man believes unto righteousness (the inward faith laying hold of the righteousness), and with the mouth confession is made unto salvation (the outward confession owning Christ); the heart-faith the root of justification, the mouth-confession its open avowal; faith and confession distinguished yet joined, the inward grace and its outward profession. The witnesses note heart believes unto righteousness. The witnesses note mouth confesses unto salvation.

The witnesses note heart-faith unto righteousness and mouth-confession unto salvation — the inward grace and its outward avowal.

So the witnesses make the verse the heart's faith and the mouth's confession. The lesson the expositors draw is that the heart-faith is the root of justification and the mouth-confession its open avowal — faith and confession distinguished yet joined. The witnesses note the heart's faith: "with the heart man believeth unto righteousness" — Gill notes true faith lies not in the bare assent of the mind, nor the brain, nor the tongue, but in the heart — a believing with all the heart; the heart's faith; the inward believing; the faith of the whole soul; the believing unto righteousness. The expositors observe the righteousness: "unto righteousness" — the heart's faith lays hold of the righteousness (of Christ, imputed); the faith unto justification; the believing that receives the righteousness; the inward faith justifying; the righteousness by faith. The witnesses press the mouth's confession: "with the mouth confession is made unto salvation" — the confession with the mouth (owning Christ) is made unto salvation; the outward confession; the avowal; the public owning; the confession unto salvation. The expositors draw the lesson on the heart-faith as the root: the heart's faith is the root — the inward grace that justifies; the believing unto righteousness; the faith the source; the root of salvation; the inward grace; "with the heart man believeth." The witnesses observe the confession as the avowal: the mouth's confession is the open avowal of the inward faith — the public owning of Christ; the confession the fruit; the avowal of the faith; the profession; the outward expression of the inward grace. The expositors note the distinction yet union: faith and confession are distinguished (the inward and the outward) yet joined (the true faith confesses, the confession springs from faith); the distinction; the union; the heart and the mouth; the root and the fruit; the inward grace and its profession. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the heart believes unto righteousness, the mouth confesses unto salvation, and the inward grace and its avowal. The verse thus records the heart's faith and the mouth's confession — teaching that the heart-faith is the root of justification and the mouth-confession its open avowal, faith and confession distinguished yet joined.
11:
"For the Scripture saith, Whosoever believeth on him shall not be ashamed." The witnesses note the Scripture promise to every believer. Gill notes the passage referred to (Isaiah 28:16, cited before in Romans 9:33) is produced to prove the connection between faith and righteousness, confession and salvation; in the prophet it is "he that believeth," but here a universal "whosoever" is added. The witnesses note Paul citing the Scripture — whosoever believes on Christ shall not be ashamed; the promise of the gospel that no believer shall be put to shame (his faith never disappointed, his hope never confounded), and the universal "whosoever" extending the promise to all who believe, Jew and Gentile alike; the security and breadth of the promise. The witnesses note whosoever believeth. The witnesses note shall not be ashamed.

The witnesses note the promise — whosoever believes shall not be ashamed; the universal "whosoever" extending the promise to all believers.

So the witnesses make the verse the Scripture promise to every believer. The lesson the expositors draw is that no believer shall be put to shame, and the universal "whosoever" extends the promise to all. The witnesses note the citation: "the Scripture saith, Whosoever believeth on him shall not be ashamed" — Gill notes the passage (Isaiah 28:16, cited in Romans 9:33) proves the connection between faith and salvation; the Scripture; the promise; the cited word; the prophet's testimony. The expositors observe the promise: "shall not be ashamed" — the believer shall not be ashamed — not put to shame, not disappointed, not confounded; his faith never disappointed; his hope never confounded; the promise of no shame; the believer secure. The witnesses press the universal "whosoever": Gill notes that in the prophet it is "he that believeth," but here a universal "whosoever" is added — extending the promise to all who believe; the universal; the whosoever; the breadth; the promise to all; the Jew and Gentile alike. The expositors draw the lesson on the security of the believer: no believer shall be ashamed — his faith resting on Christ, who never fails; the security; the faith never disappointed; the hope never confounded; the believer not put to shame; "they that trust in him shall not be confounded." The witnesses observe the breadth of "whosoever": the "whosoever" extends the promise to all who believe — Jew and Gentile, every believer; the universal offer; the breadth; the gospel for all; the promise to every believer; the no-distinction (v.12). The expositors note the contrast with the self-righteous: the self-righteous (v.3) shall be ashamed (their own righteousness failing); but the believer shall not be ashamed (Christ's righteousness never failing); the contrast; the shame of the self-righteous and the security of the believer; the failing works and the sure faith. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the whosoever believeth, the shall not be ashamed, and the universal promise. The verse thus records the Scripture promise — whosoever believes shall not be ashamed — teaching that no believer shall be put to shame, the universal "whosoever" extending the promise to all who believe.
12:
"For there is no difference between the Jew and the Greek: for the same Lord over all is rich unto all that call upon him." The witnesses note no difference between Jew and Greek, the same Lord rich to all. Gill notes there is no distinction of nations, no superiority by carnal descent or fleshly privileges, no preeminence by the former dispensation's ordinances — all now abolished; nor difference in their state Godward, all under sin and needing Christ's righteousness. The witnesses note Paul declaring that there is no difference between Jew and Greek, for the same Lord over all is rich unto all who call upon him; the breaking down of the Jew/Gentile distinction in the gospel, the one Lord (Christ) over all, abounding in mercy and grace to all who call on him; the universal lordship and the universal riches of Christ. The witnesses note no difference Jew and Greek. The witnesses note the same Lord rich to all.

The witnesses note no difference between Jew and Greek — the same Lord rich to all who call; the universal lordship and riches of Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse no difference between Jew and Greek. The lesson the expositors draw is the breaking down of the Jew/Gentile distinction in the gospel, the one Lord rich to all who call. The witnesses note the no difference: "there is no difference between the Jew and the Greek" — Gill notes no distinction of nations, no superiority by descent or privilege, no preeminence by the old ordinances (all abolished), no difference in their state Godward (all under sin); the no difference; the equality; the distinction broken; the Jew and Greek alike. The expositors observe the same Lord: "the same Lord over all" — the same Lord (Christ) is over all — Jew and Gentile; the one Lord; the universal lordship; Christ over all; the Lord of all. The witnesses press the riches to all: "is rich unto all that call upon him" — the same Lord is rich (abounding in mercy, grace, salvation) unto all who call upon him; the riches of Christ; the abundance; the bounty; the riches to all who call; the wealth of grace. The expositors draw the lesson on the breaking down of the distinction: the gospel breaks down the Jew/Gentile distinction — the same Lord, the same riches, the same way (calling on him) for all; the distinction abolished; the middle wall broken (Ephesians 2:14); the one way for all; the equality in the gospel. The witnesses observe the universal lordship: Christ is "Lord over all" — over Jew and Gentile, over all nations; the universal lordship; Christ the Lord of all; the sovereignty over all; the one Lord of the whole; the lordship universal. The expositors note the riches of Christ: Christ is "rich" — rich in mercy, grace, and salvation; the unsearchable riches (Ephesians 3:8); the abundance for all who call; the wealth of Christ poured out; the riches to the calling. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the no difference, the same Lord rich to all, and the universal riches of Christ. The verse thus records no difference between Jew and Greek — the same Lord rich to all — teaching the breaking down of the distinction and the universal lordship and riches of Christ to all who call.
13:
"For whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved." The witnesses note the promise to all who call on the Lord. Gill and Barnes note this testimony is taken from Joel 2:32, applied to the times of the gospel (as Peter in Acts 2:21); all who call on the name of the Lord shall be saved. The witnesses note Paul citing Joel — whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved; the universal promise of salvation to all who call on the Lord (in faith and prayer), with the striking fact that the "Lord" of Joel (Yahweh) is here applied to Christ — a strong testimony to his deity; the universal call and the divine Saviour. The witnesses note whosoever shall call. The witnesses note the name of the Lord applied to Christ.

The witnesses note the promise — whosoever calls on the name of the Lord shall be saved; the universal call, with "Lord" (Yahweh) applied to Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the promise to all who call on the Lord. The lesson the expositors draw is the universal promise of salvation to all who call, with the "Lord" of Joel applied to Christ — a testimony to his deity. The witnesses note the citation: "whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved" — Gill and Barnes note this is from Joel 2:32, applied to the gospel times (as Peter, Acts 2:21); the cited promise; the universal offer; the call and the salvation; Joel's word. The expositors observe the universal "whosoever": "whosoever shall call" — whosoever, without distinction, who calls on the Lord shall be saved; the universal; the no-distinction (v.12); the all who call; the breadth; the open promise. The witnesses press the calling: "call upon the name of the Lord" — to call on the name of the Lord is to invoke him in faith and prayer — to seek his salvation, to trust and pray to him; the calling; the invoking; the faith and prayer; the seeking of the Lord; the call of the believer. The expositors draw the lesson on the universal promise: whosoever calls shall be saved — the universal promise; the salvation to all who call; the open door; the gospel for all who seek; the promise to every caller; the salvation offered. The witnesses observe the striking application to Christ: the "Lord" of Joel (Yahweh, the LORD) is here applied to Christ — for it is on Christ the Lord that men call to be saved (v.9, 12); the divine name applied to Christ; the testimony to his deity; the Lord of Joel is Christ; the strong proof of his Godhead. The expositors note the deity of Christ implied: that men are saved by calling on Christ as Lord (the Yahweh of Joel) implies his deity — for to call on the Lord for salvation is to call on God; the deity of Christ; the divine Saviour; the Lord who saves is God; the Godhead of the Saviour. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the whosoever shall call, the name of the Lord applied to Christ, and the universal promise. The verse thus records the promise — whosoever calls on the name of the Lord shall be saved — teaching the universal promise of salvation, with the "Lord" of Joel applied to Christ, a testimony to his deity.
14:
"How then shall they call on him in whom they have not believed? and how shall they believe in him of whom they have not heard? and how shall they hear without a preacher?" The witnesses note the chain of preaching necessary to faith. Chrysostom notes Paul here takes from Israel all excuse — having said they had a zeal but not according to knowledge, and were ignorant, he shows that for this ignorance itself they were punishable, framing the passage out of questions and answers. The witnesses note Paul tracing the chain back — they cannot call on one they have not believed, nor believe in one they have not heard, nor hear without a preacher; the divinely-ordained sequence by which faith comes — calling rests on believing, believing on hearing, hearing on preaching; the necessity of the preached gospel for faith. The witnesses note the chain of faith. The witnesses note the necessity of the preacher.

The witnesses note the chain — calling rests on believing, believing on hearing, hearing on preaching; the preached gospel necessary to faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the chain of preaching necessary to faith. The lesson the expositors draw is the divinely-ordained sequence by which faith comes — calling resting on believing, believing on hearing, hearing on preaching. The witnesses note the chain: "how shall they call on him in whom they have not believed? and how shall they believe in him of whom they have not heard? and how shall they hear without a preacher?" — the chain traced back; calling, believing, hearing, preaching; the sequence; the links; the order of faith. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note on the purpose: Paul takes from Israel all excuse — having said they had zeal without knowledge and were ignorant, he shows they were punishable for the ignorance itself; the removal of excuse; the framing in questions; the convicting of Israel; the responsibility established. The witnesses press the sequence: calling rests on believing (one cannot call on one not believed in); believing on hearing (one cannot believe one not heard of); hearing on preaching (one cannot hear without a preacher); the divinely-ordained sequence; the chain of faith; the order of the means. The expositors draw the lesson on the necessity of the preached gospel: faith comes through the preached gospel — the necessity of preaching; the gospel must be preached, heard, believed, before it can be called on; the means of faith; the preached word necessary; the ordained sequence; "faith cometh by hearing" (v.17). The witnesses observe the necessity of the preacher: "how shall they hear without a preacher?" — the preacher necessary; the gospel must be proclaimed; the sent preacher (v.15); the means of hearing; the herald necessary; the preaching ordained. The expositors note the missionary import: the chain establishes the necessity of missions — that the gospel be carried to those who have not heard, that they may believe and call; the missionary mandate; the necessity of sending preachers; the gospel to the nations; the chain demanding the mission. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the chain of faith, the necessity of the preacher, and the ordained sequence. The verse thus records the chain of preaching necessary to faith — teaching the divinely-ordained sequence by which faith comes: calling on believing, believing on hearing, hearing on preaching.
15:
"And how shall they preach, except they be sent? as it is written, How beautiful are the feet of them that preach the gospel of peace, and bring glad tidings of good things!" The witnesses note the necessity of the sending and the beauty of the gospel messengers. Gill notes there is no proper, lawful preaching without a mission — either extraordinary (as the apostles', immediately by Christ, with miraculous gifts) or ordinary (of pastors and teachers, sent by Christ through the church). The witnesses note Paul completing the chain — how shall they preach unless they are sent? — and citing Isaiah on the beauty of the feet of those who bring the gospel of peace and glad tidings; the necessity of the divine sending of preachers, and the welcome beauty of the gospel messengers who bring the good news of peace. The witnesses note except they be sent. The witnesses note the beautiful feet.

The witnesses note the necessity of the sending and the beauty of the gospel messengers — the feet of those who bring the good news of peace.

So the witnesses make the verse the sending and the beauty of the messengers. The lesson the expositors draw is the necessity of the divine sending of preachers and the welcome beauty of the gospel messengers. The witnesses note the necessity of sending: "how shall they preach, except they be sent?" — Gill notes no lawful preaching without a mission — extraordinary (the apostles', by Christ, with miraculous gifts) or ordinary (pastors and teachers, sent by Christ through the church); the necessity of sending; the divine commission; the sent preacher; the mission required. The expositors observe the citation: "How beautiful are the feet of them that preach the gospel of peace, and bring glad tidings of good things!" — Paul cites Isaiah (52:7) on the beauty of the feet of the messengers; the beautiful feet; the welcome messengers; the gospel of peace; the glad tidings. The witnesses press the beauty of the messengers: the "feet" of the gospel messengers are "beautiful" — welcome, lovely, a delight; for they bring the good news; the beauty; the welcome; the loveliness of the messengers; the feet that bring the gospel. The expositors draw the lesson on the gospel of peace and glad tidings: the messengers preach "the gospel of peace" (reconciliation with God) and "bring glad tidings of good things" (the good news of salvation); the gospel of peace; the glad tidings; the good news; the message of reconciliation; the welcome word. The witnesses observe the necessity of the divine sending: the preacher must be sent — by Christ, through the church; the divine sending; the lawful mission; the commission; the herald sent by the King; the sending necessary. The expositors note the dignity of the ministry: the beauty of the messengers' feet shows the dignity and worth of the gospel ministry — the honour of those who bring the good news; the dignity; the honour of preaching; the worth of the messenger; the beauty of the calling. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the except they be sent, the beautiful feet, and the welcome messengers. The verse thus records the necessity of the sending and the beauty of the messengers — teaching the necessity of the divine sending of preachers and the welcome beauty of those who bring the gospel of peace.
16:
"But they have not all obeyed the gospel. For Esaias saith, Lord, who hath believed our report?" The witnesses note that not all obey the gospel. Gill notes that though ministers may be regularly sent and rightly preach, there is no success without the power of God attending it; men may hear and yet not obey — not cordially embrace the doctrines nor submit to the ordinances. The witnesses note Paul acknowledging that not all have obeyed the gospel, and citing Isaiah — "Lord, who hath believed our report?" — the sad fact of unbelief despite the preaching; the gospel preached yet not received by all, the prophet's lament over the few who believe; the necessity of God's power for the hearing to become believing. The witnesses note not all obeyed. The witnesses note who hath believed our report.

The witnesses note that not all obey the gospel — the prophet's lament over unbelief despite the preaching; God's power needed for faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the unbelief despite the preaching. The lesson the expositors draw is that the gospel preached is not received by all, and God's power is needed for the hearing to become believing. The witnesses note the unbelief: "they have not all obeyed the gospel" — not all have obeyed the gospel; Gill notes that though ministers preach rightly, there is no success without God's power; men may hear yet not obey; the unbelief; the not-obeying; the gospel rejected by many. The expositors observe the citation: "For Esaias saith, Lord, who hath believed our report?" — Paul cites Isaiah (53:1) — "who hath believed our report?"; the prophet's lament; the few who believe; the question of unbelief; Isaiah's complaint. The witnesses press the sad fact of unbelief: despite the preaching (the beautiful feet, the gospel of peace, v.15), not all believe — many hear yet reject; the sad fact; the unbelief despite the preaching; the gospel preached but not received; the few believers. The expositors draw the lesson on the necessity of God's power: Gill notes there is no success without the power of God attending the preaching; the preaching alone does not save; the hearing must become believing by God's power; the necessity of grace; the Spirit needed; the power of God for faith. The witnesses observe the obeying of the gospel: to "obey the gospel" is to cordially embrace its doctrines and submit to its ordinances — to receive Christ by faith; the obedience of faith (Romans 1:5); the gospel obeyed by believing; the submission of faith; the not-obeying the unbelief. The expositors note the prophet's lament fulfilled: Isaiah's lament ("who hath believed our report?") was fulfilled in the unbelief of the Jews toward the gospel; the prophecy fulfilled; the unbelief foretold; the few believers; the lament realized; the rejection prophesied. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the not all obeyed, the who hath believed our report, and the necessity of God's power. The verse thus records that not all obey the gospel — teaching the unbelief despite the preaching, the prophet's lament, and the necessity of God's power for faith.
17:
"So then faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God." The witnesses note that faith comes by hearing the word. Gill notes "hearing" is used as in the preceding verse, designing the report of the gospel, the preaching of the word, which is the means God uses to convey faith into the hearts of his people; preachers are the instruments by whom others believe. The witnesses note Paul concluding that faith comes by hearing, and hearing by the word of God; the ordained means of faith — the preaching of the gospel heard, and the hearing grounded in the word of God; faith wrought by the Spirit through the heard word, the word of God the instrument and the warrant of the preaching; the means of faith. The witnesses note faith by hearing. The witnesses note hearing by the word of God.

The witnesses note faith coming by hearing, and hearing by the word of God — the ordained means of faith, the preached word the instrument.

So the witnesses make the verse the means of faith. The lesson the expositors draw is the ordained means of faith — the preaching of the gospel heard, the hearing grounded in the word of God. The witnesses note the means: "faith cometh by hearing" — faith comes by hearing; Gill notes "hearing" is the report of the gospel, the preaching of the word, the means God uses to convey faith; the means of faith; the hearing; the preached word; the instrument. The expositors observe the ground of the hearing: "and hearing by the word of God" — the hearing is by (grounded in, comes from) the word of God; the word the source; the hearing of the word; the word of God the warrant; the message preached. The witnesses press the ordained means: faith comes through the preached gospel heard — the Spirit working faith through the heard word; the ordained means; the word the instrument; the preaching the channel; the faith by the word; "it pleased God by the foolishness of preaching to save them that believe" (1 Corinthians 1:21). The expositors draw the lesson on the instrument of faith: the word of God (preached and heard) is the instrument of faith — the Spirit using the word to work faith; the word the means; the preached gospel the channel; the faith wrought by the word; the instrument of the Spirit; the word effectual. The witnesses observe the necessity of the word: faith does not come without the word — the hearing of the gospel necessary; the word indispensable; the means appointed; the preaching required; the word the necessary means; the faith by the word alone. The expositors note the warrant of the preaching — the word of God: the hearing is by "the word of God" — the message preached is God's word, not man's; the warrant; the divine authority; the word of God preached; the message authoritative; the word that is preached God's own. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the faith by hearing, the hearing by the word of God, and the means of faith. The verse thus records faith coming by hearing the word — teaching the ordained means of faith, the preaching of the gospel heard, the word of God the instrument.
18:
"But I say, Have they not heard? Yes verily, their sound went into all the earth, and their words unto the ends of the world." The witnesses note that the gospel has indeed been heard. Barnes notes Paul replies to the objection (that men were condemned for not believing what they had not heard) — he does not deny the principle, but affirms that they had heard; the gospel had been widely proclaimed. The witnesses note Paul answering the question — have they not heard? Yes indeed; citing Psalm 19:4 (the witness of the heavens) and applying it to the gospel — their sound went into all the earth; the wide proclamation of the gospel, leaving the unbelieving without excuse, the message having reached far and wide; the universal spread of the gospel witness. The witnesses note have they not heard? The witnesses note the sound into all the earth.

The witnesses note the gospel widely heard — their sound went into all the earth; the wide proclamation leaving the unbelieving without excuse.

So the witnesses make the verse the wide proclamation of the gospel. The lesson the expositors draw is that the gospel has been widely heard, leaving the unbelieving without excuse. The witnesses note the question and answer: "Have they not heard? Yes verily" — Paul asks whether they have not heard, and answers, yes indeed; the question; the affirmation; the gospel heard; the answer to the objection. The expositors observe Barnes's note on the objection: Paul replies to the objection (that men were condemned for not believing what they had not heard) — not denying the principle, but affirming they had heard; the objection answered; the gospel proclaimed; the hearing granted; the excuse removed. The witnesses press the wide proclamation: "their sound went into all the earth, and their words unto the ends of the world" — Paul cites Psalm 19:4 (originally of the heavens' witness) and applies it to the gospel — the sound gone into all the earth; the wide proclamation; the message everywhere; the gospel spread far; the universal witness. The expositors draw the lesson on the gospel leaving without excuse: since the gospel has been widely heard, the unbelieving are without excuse — they have heard and not believed; the no-excuse; the responsibility; the unbelief inexcusable; the hearing granted, the believing refused; the guilt of unbelief. The witnesses observe the application of the psalm: Paul applies the psalm of the heavens' witness (Psalm 19) to the gospel — as the heavens' witness reaches all, so the gospel's sound has gone everywhere; the application; the psalm used; the wide witness; the gospel's spread likened to the heavens'. The expositors note the spread of the gospel: the gospel had spread widely in the apostolic age — to the known world; the wide proclamation; the message everywhere; the gospel's reach; the universal hearing; the word gone forth. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the have they not heard, the sound into all the earth, and the wide proclamation. The verse thus records that the gospel has been widely heard — teaching the wide proclamation leaving the unbelieving without excuse.
19:
"But I say, Did not Israel know? First Moses saith, I will provoke you to jealousy by them that are no people, and by a foolish nation I will anger you." The witnesses note that Israel knew, and the provoking to jealousy by the Gentiles. Gill notes Israel did know — they knew God, his will, the right worship, and even the gospel (not spiritually, but nationally and speculatively, against the light and conviction). The witnesses note Paul asking whether Israel did not know, and citing Moses (Deuteronomy 32:21) — God would provoke Israel to jealousy by "them that are no people" (the Gentiles) and anger them by a foolish nation; Israel's knowledge of the gospel and the coming call of the Gentiles, foretold by Moses, that the Gentiles' reception of the grace Israel refused would provoke them to jealousy; Israel inexcusable and the Gentiles called. The witnesses note did not Israel know? The witnesses note provoked to jealousy by the Gentiles.

The witnesses note Israel knew, and Moses foretold the provoking to jealousy by the Gentiles — Israel inexcusable, the Gentiles called.

So the witnesses make the verse Israel's knowledge and the provoking to jealousy. The lesson the expositors draw is that Israel knew (and so was inexcusable), and that the call of the Gentiles, foretold by Moses, would provoke Israel to jealousy. The witnesses note the question: "Did not Israel know?" — Paul asks whether Israel did not know; Gill notes Israel did know — God, his will, the right worship, even the gospel (nationally, against the light); the knowledge; the not-ignorance; Israel knew. The expositors observe the citation of Moses: "First Moses saith, I will provoke you to jealousy by them that are no people, and by a foolish nation I will anger you" — Paul cites Moses (Deuteronomy 32:21); God would provoke Israel to jealousy by "them that are no people" (the Gentiles) and anger them by a foolish nation; Moses's word; the prophecy; the provoking foretold. The witnesses press the provoking to jealousy: God would provoke Israel to jealousy by the Gentiles — that the Gentiles' reception of the grace Israel refused would stir Israel to jealousy; the jealousy; the Gentiles' call provoking Israel; the design of mercy (Romans 11:11); the provoking for Israel's good. The expositors draw the lesson on Israel inexcusable: since Israel knew (the gospel, and the foretold call of the Gentiles), they were inexcusable in their unbelief; the no-excuse; the knowledge aggravating; the responsibility; Israel without excuse; the guilt of the knowing. The witnesses observe the call of the Gentiles foretold: Moses foretold the call of the Gentiles ("them that are no people," "a foolish nation") — the Gentiles, once no people of God, brought in; the Gentiles' call prophesied; the no-people made a people (Hosea 2:23); the foretold mercy; the Gentiles received. The expositors note the design of the jealousy: the provoking to jealousy was designed for Israel's good — that, seeing the Gentiles blessed, Israel might be stirred to seek the grace they refused; the design of mercy; the jealousy for restoration; the provoking toward repentance; the mercy in the provoking (Romans 11). The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the did not Israel know, the provoked to jealousy, and the call of the Gentiles. The verse thus records Israel's knowledge and the provoking to jealousy — teaching that Israel knew and was inexcusable, and the call of the Gentiles, foretold by Moses, would provoke Israel to jealousy.
20:
"But Esaias is very bold, and saith, I was found of them that sought me not; I was made manifest unto them that asked not after me." The witnesses note Isaiah's bold prophecy of the Gentiles' call. Gill notes Isaiah was "very bold" — he did not mince the matter or cover it with dark sayings, but with all plainness, courage, and intrepidity declared the calling of the Gentiles, though he ran the risk of his credit and even his life among the Jews. The witnesses note Paul citing Isaiah (65:1) — God was found of them that sought him not, made manifest to them that asked not after him; the sovereign grace that found the Gentiles who did not seek God, calling those who never sought him; the boldness of the prophet in declaring the unsought mercy to the Gentiles; the grace that seeks the unseeking. The witnesses note found of them that sought me not. The witnesses note the boldness of Isaiah.

The witnesses note Isaiah's bold prophecy — God found of them that sought him not; the sovereign grace that seeks the unseeking Gentiles.

So the witnesses make the verse Isaiah's bold prophecy of the Gentiles' call. The lesson the expositors draw is the sovereign grace that found the Gentiles who did not seek God, and the boldness of the prophet in declaring it. The witnesses note the boldness: "Esaias is very bold" — Gill notes Isaiah was very bold — not mincing the matter nor covering it with dark sayings, but with plainness, courage, and intrepidity declaring the calling of the Gentiles, though he risked his credit and life; the boldness; the courage; the plain declaration; the intrepid prophet. The expositors observe the citation: "I was found of them that sought me not; I was made manifest unto them that asked not after me" — Paul cites Isaiah (65:1); God was found of them that sought him not, made manifest to them that asked not after him; the Gentiles found by God; the unsought mercy; the grace to the unseeking. The witnesses press the sovereign grace: God was "found of them that sought me not" — the Gentiles, who did not seek God, were found by him; the sovereign grace; the unsought mercy; God seeking the unseeking; the grace that finds those who sought not; the prevenient grace. The expositors draw the lesson on the grace that seeks the unseeking: God found the Gentiles who never sought him — the sovereign, prevenient grace; the grace before the seeking; the God who seeks the lost; the mercy unsought; "we love him, because he first loved us" (1 John 4:19); the grace that finds. The witnesses observe the manifestation to the unasking: God was "made manifest unto them that asked not after me" — revealed to those who did not ask; the manifestation; the revelation to the unasking; the grace that reveals to those who sought not; the mercy unmerited; the God who manifests himself. The expositors note the contrast with Israel: the Gentiles, who sought not, found God; Israel, who had the oracles, did not submit (v.21); the contrast; the unseeking found, the privileged refusing; the irony; the Gentiles received, Israel rejecting; the reversal. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the found of them that sought me not, the boldness of Isaiah, and the sovereign grace. The verse thus records Isaiah's bold prophecy of the Gentiles' call — teaching the sovereign grace that found the Gentiles who did not seek God.
21:
"But to Israel he saith, All day long I have stretched forth my hands unto a disobedient and gainsaying people." The witnesses note God's patient pleading with disobedient Israel. Barnes notes that while the Gentiles would be obedient, the character of the Jews was that they were a disobedient and rebellious people; "all day long" means continually, without intermission. The witnesses note Paul citing Isaiah (65:2) against Israel — all day long God stretched out his hands to a disobedient and gainsaying people; the image of God's patient, persistent pleading (the outstretched hands of invitation and entreaty) met by Israel's continual disobedience and contradiction; the long-suffering of God and the obstinacy of Israel, closing the chapter on the tragedy of the privileged people's unbelief. The witnesses note stretched forth my hands. The witnesses note a disobedient people.

The witnesses note God all day long stretching out his hands to disobedient Israel — the patient pleading met by continual disobedience.

So the witnesses make the verse God's patient pleading with disobedient Israel. The lesson the expositors draw is the long-suffering of God, the outstretched hands of invitation met by Israel's continual disobedience. The witnesses note the pleading: "All day long I have stretched forth my hands" — God stretched out his hands all day long; the outstretched hands of invitation, entreaty, and welcome; the patient pleading; the persistent appeal; the hands held out. The expositors observe the "all day long": Barnes notes "all day long" means continually, without intermission; the constant pleading; the persistent appeal; the long-suffering; the unceasing invitation; the patience prolonged. The witnesses press the image of the outstretched hands: the outstretched hands picture God's patient pleading — the gesture of invitation, entreaty, and longing welcome; the hands of mercy held out; the appeal of love; the God who pleads; the entreaty of grace. The expositors draw the lesson on the long-suffering of God: God's all-day-long stretching out of his hands shows his long-suffering — his patient, persistent appeal to Israel; the long-suffering; the patience; the God who waits and pleads; "how often would I have gathered... and ye would not!" (Matthew 23:37); the patient God. The witnesses observe Israel's disobedience: "unto a disobedient and gainsaying people" — Israel was disobedient (refusing to obey) and gainsaying (contradicting, opposing); the disobedience; the contradiction; the obstinacy; the rebellion; the resisting of the pleading God. The expositors note the tragedy of the privileged people: the chapter closes on the tragedy — the privileged people (with the oracles, the covenants, the pleading God) disobedient and gainsaying, while the unseeking Gentiles found mercy (v.20); the tragedy; the privileged refusing; the obstinacy of Israel; the long-suffering met by rebellion; the sad close. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the stretched forth my hands, the disobedient people, and the patient pleading of God. The verse thus records God's patient pleading with disobedient Israel — teaching the long-suffering of God, the outstretched hands of invitation met by Israel's continual disobedience, the tragedy of the privileged people's unbelief.
